one_line_takeaway: WorldCup reframes multi-bit LLM watermarking as a hierarchical tournament-sampling communication channel with complementary g-value functions and entropy-aware, confidence-weighted decoding, empirically beating MPAC, SegMark, StealthInk, and BiMark on bit accuracy, text quality (PPL), decoding speed, and robustness to word/copy-paste/paraphrase attacks across 16-48 bit payloads on LLaMA3-8B and Gemma2-9B.
key_finding: On Table 1 (LLaMA3-8B-Base/Gemma2-9B-Base, AUC/Bit Acc/PPL/decode-time), WorldCup matches or exceeds the strongest baseline (BiMark) on AUC while improving bit accuracy and reducing PPL and decode time at every tested payload; e.g. at 16 bits/256 tokens on LLaMA3-8B, WorldCup reaches AUC 1.000, Bit Acc 0.990, PPL 11.25, decode time 0.015s vs. BiMark's AUC 1.000, Bit Acc 0.987, PPL 11.25, time 0.044s, and vs. MPAC's Bit Acc 0.980, PPL 13.56, time 0.027s. The paper explicitly reports that on LLaMA3-8B, WorldCup improves bit accuracy over StealthInk by 3.2%, 4.8%, 5.5%, and 5.2% at 16/24/32/48 bits respectively, and that decode time is consistently the lowest of all methods (0.008-0.017s vs. e.g. SegMark's 0.55-3.38s). Under robustness attacks at 16-bit/256-token settings (Fig. 4), WorldCup attains average AUC 98.9% and decoding accuracy 91.1%, exceeding BiMark, MPAC, StealthInk, and SegMark by 3.2%, 6.7%, 14%, and 15.1% respectively. In downstream long-form QA, WorldCup surpasses MPAC's Bit Accuracy by an average of 2.6%, 2.7%, and 3.0% on Ministral-8B-IT, LLaMA3.1-8B-IT, and Gemma2-9B-IT respectively (Table 2). Ablations show the entropy-aware factor trades a small amount of decoding accuracy for substantially lower PPL, while removing the complementary-distribution subtraction lowers PPL but sharply degrades decoding accuracy, and complementary g-value functions consistently beat randomly paired ones on both accuracy and PPL. Increasing k from 1 to 8 increases per-token encoding time roughly linearly (0.08s to 0.69s at 256 tokens) but does not monotonically improve accuracy -- the paper states k=2 is typically best in practice.
core_contribution: The paper proposes WorldCup, a multi-bit LLM watermarking framework that reframes token sampling itself as a structured communication channel rather than adding a static logit bias on top of a zero-bit scheme. It (1) generalizes SynthID-Text's binary tournament sampling to a multi-bit setting via two independent families of pseudo-random 'g-value' functions per bit, chosen to be exact complements (g and 1-g) of each other, which the authors prove (Proposition 3.5, proof in Appendix G.1) maximizes statistical discriminability between the bit-0 and bit-1 embedding distributions; (2) generalizes this to k groups of g-value functions per token so each token can carry k bits, combining per-group scores additively and then subtracting the complementary-distribution contribution to sharpen separation, modulated by an entropy-aware factor lambda = alpha * sigma(entropy of the base LLM distribution) so that watermark strength is reduced at low-entropy (confident) token positions to protect fluency; and (3) replaces prior counting-based (majority-vote) multi-bit decoding with a confidence-aware decoder that averages continuous g-value scores per message position/group rather than making hard per-token green/red decisions, and uses this to derive a z-score statistic for zero-bit-style watermark detection.
threat_model: The setting is inference-time LLM watermarking for content attribution/traceability, not an adversarial-attack paper against a defended system. The 'adversary' is a downstream party attempting to strip or corrupt the embedded watermark message via text perturbations: word-level edits (deletion, synonym substitution), copy-paste splicing, and paraphrasing with the Dipper paraphraser (tested at two lexical/order-diversity settings), evaluated as AUROC/bit-accuracy degradation under attack (Section 4.4, Fig. 4). There is no adversary attempting model extraction, prompt injection, or watermark forgery/spoofing analysis in the main text; robustness is measured only against these three families of post-hoc text-perturbation attacks with the watermark key/method otherwise assumed secret and uncompromised.
limitations: The paper's own text does not include a dedicated Limitations section within the pages captured; noted caveats stated in-text: (1) decoding accuracy on Gemma2-9B-Base is consistently lower than on LLaMA3-8B-Base across all payload sizes (drops of 7.1%, 5.3%, 8.0%, and 5.2% at 16/24/32/48 bits respectively), attributed loosely to 'token-level uncertainty' with details deferred to an appendix; (2) capacity beyond k=2 groups does not reliably improve accuracy because per-token information capacity is limited, and larger k linearly increases encoding cost; (3) larger hash-window sizes reduce robustness by weakening the locality of watermark signal aggregation (per Appendix I.6, referenced but not detailed in the captured pages); (4) robustness is only evaluated against word-edit, copy-paste, and Dipper-paraphrase attacks -- no evaluation against adaptive/watermark-aware adversaries, model distillation, or cross-model transfer attacks appears in the captured sections; (5) the method assumes the tournament-sampling approximation (2^m >> |V|) and a vectorized equivalent to the literal Algorithm 1 tournament, with the equivalence proof deferred to an appendix not reviewed here.

datasets_used: machine translation task (WMT-style, per baselines section)
quantitative_results: 16-bit/256-token LLaMA3-8B-Base: WorldCup AUC 1.000, Bit Acc 0.990, PPL 11.25, decode time 0.015s
48-bit/256-token LLaMA3-8B-Base: WorldCup AUC 1.000, Bit Acc 0.916, PPL 11.63, decode time 0.017s
Bit accuracy improvement over StealthInk on LLaMA3-8B: +3.2%/+4.8%/+5.5%/+5.2% at 16/24/32/48 bits
Under combined attacks (16-bit, 256 tokens, LLaMA3-8B): WorldCup avg AUC 98.9%, Bit Acc 91.1%, beating BiMark/MPAC/StealthInk/SegMark by 3.2%/6.7%/14%/15.1%
Long-form QA Bit Accuracy improvement over MPAC: +2.6% (Ministral-8B-IT), +2.7% (LLaMA3.1-8B-IT), +3.0% (Gemma2-9B-IT)
PPL reduction from 128->256 tokens for WorldCup: -2.01 (LLaMA3-8B), -1.82 (Gemma2-9B)
Encoding time vs. k groups (256-token sequences): 0.08s at k=1 up to 0.69s at k=8; best accuracy typically at k=2
Zero-bit detection: WorldCup average AUC ~99.7% across all scenarios
baselines_compared: SynthID-Text (zero-bit foundation, described but not a direct empirical baseline in main tables)
